title: Labor's new homes numbers $14b off RBA's
full_text:
The federal budget forecasts about $13.7 billion more investment in new housing over the next two years than the Reserve Bank does, in a split economists say is unusually large and could mean the Albanese government has overestimated new housing supply by more than 10,000 homes.
The RBA forecasts do not include the 35,000 fewer homes the government expects over the next decade from its changes to negative gearing and the capital gains tax discount, which economists say makes the discrepancy even more puzzling and highlights the challenges of meeting Labor's behind-schedule 1.2 million new homes target.
The RBA forecasts, published a week before the budget, show about $301 billion in dwelling investment in 2026-27 and 2027-28. In the budget on May 12, Treasury forecast $315 billion investment over the same period.
"This is quite a large divergence, especially considering Treasury's forecasts also take into account the headwind to new housing supply created by the negative gearing and capital gains tax changes," said Trent Wiltshire, the chief economist with construction consultancy Rider Levett Bucknall. Wiltshire said the budget presented a more rosy picture for the economy across the board and that while differences in forecasts were normal, the housing investment gap stood out as "unusual".
Treasury expects Labor's negative gearing and CGT changes to reduce housing supply by 35,000 homes over the next decade.
The government has faced intense calls from the business community to restrict the changes to property investments and for carve-outs for small business and start-ups. The entire tax package will face a three-week Senate inquiry in June, setting up a three-way fight between the Coalition, the Greens and the government.
Deloitte Access Economics partner Stephen Smith said he did not expect the tax changes to have a material impact on dwelling supply in the near term. But if Treasury disagreed and expected a more immediate impact, that would make the gap "even more difficult to understand", Smith said.
From late 2027, the RBA's forecasts show dwelling investment falling while Treasury continues to expect strong growth.
"The differences between Treasury's economic forecasts and those of the RBA are significant and hard to reconcile," Smith said.
"The RBA appears to be taking account of current market conditions and constraints in the construction sector to a greater extent than Treasury."
Challenger chief economist and RBA veteran Jonathan Kearns estimated that if the RBA's investment forecasts were correct, about 10,000 fewer homes could be built over the next two years.
He said Treasury's housing forecasts were "broadly consistent" with its economic forecasts, but the size of the difference was still surprising.
"They don't have to agree, but they're using pretty similar [economic] framework, similar assumptions, and they're both very well resourced. So it is surprising when they come out with fairly different output," he said.
"A large part of it is just the different outlook for the economy that the Treasury is generally more bullish."
Treasurer Jim Chalmers' office did not respond to a request for comment.
The budget says the decline in housing supply from the government's tax changes will be more than offset by a 65,000-home boost over a decade from other initiatives such as its $2 billion fund for housing infrastructure.
"Our government is unapologetically pro-supply. If we want housing to be more affordable for Australians, we need to build, build, build," Housing Minister Clare O'Neill said at the National Press Club on Thursday.
Smith said these measures were unlikely to add significantly to dwelling supply over the next two years, so do not explain the difference between Treasury and the RBA's forecasts.
"This budget was built on a house of broken promises and can't even get its housing forecasts right," Shadow Treasurer Tim Wilson said.
"This budget is running a freight train through new housing construction through higher taxes, and Australians will be taxed more, pay more on their mortgages and have less money in their hip pocket."
Kearns said expanding housing supply was a more effective way of improving affordability than tax changes.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Banking and Economy
